24	\section{Setup and motivation}
25	\label{sec:ab_setup}
26	
27	A regular projective surface \(S\) over a field --- such as \(\mathbb{P}^1 \times \mathbb{P}^1\)
28	or the product \(C \times C\) of a smooth projective curve \(C\) with itself --- has a regular
29	local ring at every (closed) point, of Krull dimension \(2\). To extend a rational map
30	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from \(S\) to an abelian variety across a codimension-\(2\) closed
31	point, one argues that the local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) at such a point has \emph{depth at
32	least} \(2\), hence a rational function with no codimension-\(1\) poles on a neighbourhood of \(x\)
33	extends across \(x\). The depth \(\geq 2\) inequality is exactly what the
34	Auslander--Buchsbaum formula gives: for a regular local ring \(R\) one has \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\),
35	so
36	\[
37	  \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{pd}_R(R) + \text{depth}(R) \;=\; \text{depth}(R)
38	\]
39	trivially, but combined with the regular-sequence definition of depth and the fact that a
40	regular local ring of dimension \(d\) admits an \(R\)-regular sequence of length \(d\) generating
41	the maximal ideal, one obtains \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\), i.e.\ \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay
42	(Corollary~REF below). This is the input A.4.a needs.
43	
44	This chapter packages:
45	\begin{enumerate}
46	  \item the regular-sequence definition of depth and its \(\text{Ext}\) reformulation
47	    (Definition~REF and Lemma~REF),
48	  \item the projective-dimension invariant of a finitely-generated module
49	    (Definition~REF),
50	  \item the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma
51	    (Lemma~REF),
52	  \item the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula proper
53	    (Theorem~REF), and
54	  \item the corollary regular \(\Rightarrow\) Cohen--Macaulay
55	    (Corollary~REF).
56	\end{enumerate}
57	A short closing paragraph (Section~REF) explains how A.4.a
58	consumes Corollary~REF to push a codim-1 extension across a
59	codim-2 point.
60	
61	\section{Depth of a module over a local ring}
62	\label{sec:ab_depth}
63	
64	\begin{definition}
65	
66	\leanok
67	  [Depth of a module]
68	  \label{def:depth}
69	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth}
70	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth)
71	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17748-L17759).
72	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
73	  % "Let \(R\) be a ring, and \(I \subset R\) an ideal. Let \(M\) be a finite \(R\)-module.
74	  %  The {\it \(I\)-depth} of \(M\), denoted \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is defined as follows:
75	  %  \begin{enumerate}
76	  %  \item if \(IM \not = M\), then \(\text{depth}_I(M)\) is the supremum in
77	  %  \(\{0, 1, 2, \ldots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences in \(I\),
78	  %  \item if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\).
79	  %  \end{enumerate}
80	  %  If \((R, \mathfrak m)\) is local we call \(\text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M)\) simply
81	  %  the {\it depth} of \(M\)."
82	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LF (definition-depth).}
83	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(I \subseteq R\) be an ideal, and let \(M\) be a finitely
84	  generated \(R\)-module. The \emph{\(I\)-depth} of \(M\), written \(\text{depth}_I(M)\), is the
85	  supremum in \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths of \(M\)-regular sequences contained
86	  in \(I\), provided \(IM \neq M\); if \(IM = M\) we set \(\text{depth}_I(M) = \infty\). When
87	  \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is a Noetherian local ring we abbreviate
88	  \(\text{depth}(M) := \text{depth}_{\mathfrak{m}}(M)\) and call this simply the
89	  \emph{depth} of \(M\). The depth of the ring \(R\) itself is \(\text{depth}(R)\), the depth
90	  of \(R\) as a module over itself.
91	\end{definition}
92	
93	Recall that a sequence \(f_1, \dots, f_r \in R\) is called \emph{\(M\)-regular} if
94	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_r)M \neq 0\) and for each \(i\), the element \(f_i\) is a non-zero-divisor on
95	\(M/(f_1, \dots, f_{i-1})M\). The empty sequence is regular on \(M\) iff \(M \neq 0\) (it is
96	\emph{not} regular on the zero module, but the convention \(\text{depth}(0) = \infty\)
97	above is the usual one).
98	
99	The two practical computations of depth are the regular-sequence definition (used to
100	establish a \emph{lower} bound) and the \(\text{Ext}\) characterisation (used to establish an
101	\emph{upper} bound and to prove the depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence inequality).
102	
103	\begin{lemma}
104	
105	\leanok
106	  [Depth via \(\text{Ext}\)]
107	  \label{lem:depth_via_ext}
108	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_eq_smallest_ext_index}
109	  
110	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext)
111	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17845-L17885).
112	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
113	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring with maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\).
114	  %  Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module. Then \(\text{depth}(M)\)
115	  %  is equal to the smallest integer \(i\) such that
116	  %  \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\) is nonzero."
117	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LP (lemma-depth-ext).}
118	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring with residue field
119	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak{m}\) and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely generated \(R\)-module. Then
120	  \[
121	    \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \inf\bigl\{\, i \in \mathbb{N} \;\bigm|\; \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \,\bigr\}.
122	  \]
123	\end{lemma}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
126	% "Let \(\delta(M)\) denote the depth of \(M\) and let \(i(M)\) denote
127	%  the smallest integer \(i\) such that \(\Ext^i_R(R/\mathfrak m, M)\)
128	%  is nonzero. We will see in a moment that \(i(M) < \infty\).
129	%  By Lemma REF we have
130	%  \(\delta(M) = 0\) if and only if \(i(M) = 0\), because
131	%  \(\mathfrak m \in \text{Ass}(M)\) exactly means
132	%  that \(i(M) = 0\). Hence if \(\delta(M)\) or \(i(M)\) is \(> 0\), then we may
133	%  choose \(x \in \mathfrak m\) such that (a) \(x\) is a nonzerodivisor
134	%  on \(M\), and (b) \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta(M) - 1\).
135	%  Consider the long exact sequence
136	%  of Ext-groups associated to the short exact sequence
137	%  \(0 \to M \to M \to M/xM \to 0\) by Lemma REF:
138	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
139	%  Since \(x \in \mathfrak m\) all the maps $\Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)
140	%  \to \Ext^i_R(\kappa, M)$ are zero, see
141	%  Lemma REF.
142	%  Thus it is clear that \(i(M/xM) = i(M) - 1\). Induction on
143	%  \(\delta(M)\) finishes the proof."
144	\begin{proof}
145	  
146	  
147	  
148	  
149	  Write \(\delta := \text{depth}(M)\) and \(i := \inf\{ j : \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0 \}\).
150	  The base case \(\delta = 0\) is exactly \(\text{Hom}_R(\kappa, M) \neq 0\) (some element of
151	  \(M\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\), i.e.\ \(\mathfrak{m} \in \text{Ass}(M)\), i.e.\
152	  \(\mathfrak{m}\) contains a zero-divisor on \(M\)), which is \(i = 0\). For the inductive step
153	  pick \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) a non-zero-divisor on \(M\) with
154	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \delta - 1\). The short exact sequence
155	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{x} M \to M/xM \to 0\) induces a long exact sequence of $\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa,
156	  -)\(. Multiplication by \)x\( annihilates each \)\text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)$ (because
157	  \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\) and \(\kappa\) is annihilated by \(\mathfrak{m}\)), so the long exact
158	  sequence breaks into short pieces giving
159	  \(\text{Ext}^{j-1}_R(\kappa, M/xM) \cong \text{Ext}^j_R(\kappa, M)\) at the relevant
160	  indices, and the smallest non-vanishing index drops by \(1\). By induction
161	  \(i(M/xM) = i - 1 = \delta - 1\), so \(i = \delta\).
162	\end{proof}
163	
164	\section{Projective dimension}
165	\label{sec:ab_pd}
166	
167	\begin{definition}
168	
169	\leanok
170	  [Projective dimension]
171	  \label{def:projective_dimension}
172	  \lean{Module.projectiveDimension}
173	  % NOTE: Mathlib pinned at b80f227 may already expose this as
174	  % `Module.projectiveDimension` via
175	  % `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.ProjectiveDimension` /
176	  % `Mathlib.Algebra.Homology.ProjectiveResolution`; the prover should re-export
177	  % rather than re-define if the API is present. This block fixes the
178	  % mathematical content the prover must match.
179	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module. The \emph{projective
180	  dimension} of \(M\), written \(\text{pd}_R(M)\), is the infimum in
181	  \(\{0, 1, 2, \dots, \infty\}\) of the lengths \(n\) of projective resolutions
182	  \[
183	    0 \longrightarrow P_n \longrightarrow P_{n-1} \longrightarrow \cdots
184	    \longrightarrow P_1 \longrightarrow P_0 \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
185	  \]
186	  with each \(P_i\) a projective \(R\)-module. We say \(M\) has \emph{finite projective dimension}
187	  if \(\text{pd}_R(M) < \infty\), and \emph{infinite projective dimension} otherwise. By
188	  convention \(\text{pd}_R(0) = -\infty\) (some references); we use \(\text{pd}_R(0) = 0\) here
189	  (the empty resolution \(0 \to 0\) of length \(0\)), but the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula in
190	  Theorem~REF concerns only nonzero \(M\).
191	  In the local Noetherian setting a finitely generated \(R\)-module of finite projective
192	  dimension admits a \emph{minimal finite free resolution}: a resolution with each \(P_i\)
193	  free of finite rank and each transition map \(P_{i+1} \to P_i\) landing in \(\mathfrak{m} P_i\).
194	\end{definition}
195	
196	The two basic facts about \(\text{pd}_R(M)\) needed downstream are:
197	\begin{itemize}
198	  \item \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) iff \(M\) is projective (in the local Noetherian setting,
199	    iff \(M\) is free);
200	  \item if \(0 \to K \to P \to M \to 0\) is a short exact sequence with \(P\) projective, then
201	    \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_R(K) + 1\) provided \(K \neq 0\).
202	\end{itemize}
203	Both are standard homological algebra and are recorded under
204	\texttt{Module.projectiveDimension\_succ\_iff} or similar in Mathlib.
205	
206	\section{Depth on a short exact sequence}
207	\label{sec:ab_depth_ses}
208	
209	\begin{lemma}
210	
211	\leanok
212	  [Depth on a short exact sequence]
213	  \label{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}
214	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_of_short_exact}
215	  
216	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses)
217	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L17887-L17920).
218	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
219	  % "Let \(R\) be a local Noetherian ring. Let \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\)
220	  %  be a short exact sequence of nonzero finite \(R\)-modules.
221	  %  \begin{enumerate}
222	  %  \item
223	  %  \(\text{depth}(N) \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N'), \text{depth}(N'')\}\)
224	  %  \item
225	  %  \(\text{depth}(N'') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N') - 1\}\)
226	  %  \item
227	  %  \(\text{depth}(N') \geq \min\{\text{depth}(N), \text{depth}(N'') + 1\}\)
228	  %  \end{enumerate}"
229	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LE (lemma-depth-in-ses).}
230	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let
231	  \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\) be a short exact sequence of nonzero finitely generated
232	  \(R\)-modules. Then
233	  \begin{enumerate}
234	    \item \(\text{depth}(N) \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N'),\; \text{depth}(N'')\bigr)\);
235	    \item \(\text{depth}(N'') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N') - 1\bigr)\);
236	    \item \(\text{depth}(N') \;\geq\; \min\bigl(\text{depth}(N),\; \text{depth}(N'') + 1\bigr)\).
237	  \end{enumerate}
238	\end{lemma}
239	
240	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
241	% "Use the characterization of depth using the Ext groups
242	%  \(\Ext^i(\kappa, N)\), see Lemma REF,
243	%  and use the long exact cohomology sequence
244	%  [diagram of Ext long exact sequence]
245	%  from Lemma REF."
246	\begin{proof}
247	  
248	  
249	  
250	  
251	  By \cref{lem:depth_via_ext}, \(\text{depth}(N')\) (resp.\ \(\text{depth}(N)\),
252	  \(\text{depth}(N'')\)) is the smallest index at which \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) is
253	  nonzero on \(N'\) (resp.\ \(N\), \(N''\)). Apply the long exact \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\)
254	  sequence to \(0 \to N' \to N \to N'' \to 0\):
255	  \[
256	    \cdots \to \text{Ext}^{i-1}_R(\kappa, N'')
257	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N')
258	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N)
259	    \to \text{Ext}^i_R(\kappa, N'')
260	    \to \text{Ext}^{i+1}_R(\kappa, N') \to \cdots
261	  \]
262	  Each of the three inequalities is then a direct read-off of the fact that if two
263	  of the three modules in a length-3 chunk vanish then the middle vanishes (for (1)) /
264	  the boundary terms vanish (for (2),(3)). Compare with the diagonal vanishing argument in
265	  the proof of Lemma~REF.
266	\end{proof}
267	
268	\section{The Auslander--Buchsbaum formula}
269	\label{sec:ab_main}
270	
271	\begin{theorem}
272	
273	\leanok
274	  [Auslander--Buchsbaum formula]
275	  \label{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}
276	  \lean{RingTheory.auslander_buchsbaum_formula}
277	  
278	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum)
279	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L27207-L27280).
280	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
281	  % "Let \(R\) be a Noetherian local ring. Let \(M\) be a nonzero finite \(R\)-module
282	  %  which has finite projective dimension \(\text{pd}_R(M)\). Then we have
283	  %  \(\)
284	  %  \text{depth}(R) = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)
285	  %  \(\)"
286	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 090V (proposition-Auslander-Buchsbaum); cf.\
287	  Matsumura, \emph{Commutative Ring Theory}, Theorem 19.1; Auslander--Buchsbaum,
288	  \emph{Homological dimension in local rings}, 1957.}
289	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be a Noetherian local ring and let \(M\) be a nonzero finitely
290	  generated \(R\)-module of finite projective dimension. Then
291	  \[
292	    \text{pd}_R(M) \;+\; \text{depth}(M) \;=\; \text{depth}(R).
293	  \]
294	\end{theorem}
295	
296	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
297	% "We prove this by induction on \(\text{depth}(M)\). The most interesting
298	%  case is the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\). In this case, let
299	%  \(\)
300	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
301	%  \(\)
302	%  be a minimal finite free resolution, so \(e = \text{pd}_R(M)\).
303	%  By Lemma REF we may assume all matrix
304	%  coefficients of the maps in the complex are contained in the maximal
305	%  ideal of \(R\). Then on the one hand, by
306	%  Proposition REF we see that
307	%  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, breaking the long
308	%  exact sequence into short exact sequences
309	%  \begin{align*}
310	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e - 1}} \to K_{e - 2} \to 0,\\
311	%  0 \to K_{e - 2} \to R^{n_{e - 2}} \to K_{e - 3} \to 0,\\
312	%  \ldots,\\
313	%  0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
314	%  \end{align*}
315	%  we see, using Lemma REF, that
316	%  \begin{align*}
317	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1,\\
318	%  \text{depth}(K_{e - 3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2,\\
319	%  \ldots,\\
320	%  \text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e - 1),\\
321	%  \text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e
322	%  \end{align*}
323	%  and since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we conclude \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\).
324	%  This finishes the proof of the case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).
325	%
326	%  Induction step. If \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), then we pick \(x \in \mathfrak m\)
327	%  which is a nonzerodivisor on both \(M\) and \(R\). This is possible, because
328	%  either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\) and \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the aforementioned
329	%  Proposition REF or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which
330	%  case \(M\) is finite free hence also \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\).
331	%  Thus \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by Lemma REF
332	%  (for example) and we can find an \(x \in \mathfrak m\) which is a nonzerodivisor
333	%  on both \(R\) and \(M\). Let
334	%  \(\)
335	%  0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0
336	%  \(\)
337	%  be a minimal resolution as above. An application of the snake lemma
338	%  shows that
339	%  \(\)
340	%  0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \ldots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to
341	%  M/xM \to 0
342	%  \(\)
343	%  is a minimal resolution too. Thus \(\text{pd}_R(M) = \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\).
344	%  By Lemma REF we have
345	%  \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
346	%  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\).
347	%  Till now depths have all been depths as \(R\) modules, but we observe that
348	%  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) and similarly for \(R/xR\).
349	%  By induction hypothesis we see that the
350	%  Auslander-Buchsbaum formula holds for \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\). Since the
351	%  depths of both \(R/xR\) and \(M/xM\) have decreased by one and the projective
352	%  dimension has not changed we conclude."
353	\begin{proof}
354	  
355	  
356	  We induct on \(\text{depth}(M)\).
357	
358	  \emph{Base case \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\).} Let \(e := \text{pd}_R(M)\) and pick a minimal
359	  finite free resolution
360	  \[
361	    0 \longrightarrow R^{n_e} \longrightarrow R^{n_{e-1}} \longrightarrow
362	    \cdots \longrightarrow R^{n_0} \longrightarrow M \longrightarrow 0
363	  \]
364	  with all transition maps having matrix coefficients in \(\mathfrak{m}\) (this is the
365	  ``minimal-resolution-after-trimming-trivial-summands'' normalisation, cf.\ Stacks
366	  \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}). On the one hand, the ``what is exact'' criterion
367	  (Stacks tag 00MF, \texttt{proposition-what-exact}, expressing exactness of a sequence of
368	  finite free modules in terms of depths of ideals of \(r\)-minors) gives
369	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq e\). On the other hand, splitting the resolution into short exact
370	  sequences
371	  \[
372	    0 \to R^{n_e} \to R^{n_{e-1}} \to K_{e-2} \to 0, \quad
373	    0 \to K_{e-2} \to R^{n_{e-2}} \to K_{e-3} \to 0, \quad \dots, \quad
374	    0 \to K_0 \to R^{n_0} \to M \to 0,
375	  \]
376	  and applying \cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence} iteratively,
377	  yields the chain of inequalities
378	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-2}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 1\),
379	  \(\text{depth}(K_{e-3}) \geq \text{depth}(R) - 2\), \dots,
380	  \(\text{depth}(K_0) \geq \text{depth}(R) - (e-1)\), and finally
381	  \(\text{depth}(M) \geq \text{depth}(R) - e\). Since \(\text{depth}(M) = 0\) we deduce
382	  \(\text{depth}(R) \leq e\). Combined with the previous inequality, $\text{depth}(R) = e =
383	  \text{pd}_R(M) + 0 = \text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M)$.
384	
385	  \emph{Inductive step \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\).} We claim there exists \(x \in \mathfrak{m}\)
386	  which is a non-zero-divisor on both \(R\) and \(M\). Indeed: either \(\text{pd}_R(M) > 0\), in
387	  which case \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) by the ``what is exact'' criterion applied to the start
388	  of a minimal resolution, or \(\text{pd}_R(M) = 0\) in which case \(M\) is finite free and
389	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \text{depth}(M) > 0\). Either way both \(\text{depth}(R) > 0\) and
390	  \(\text{depth}(M) > 0\), hence \(\text{depth}(R \oplus M) > 0\) by
391	  \cref{lem:depth_short_exact_sequence}, so a common non-zero-divisor $x \in
392	  \mathfrak{m}\( exists. Take a minimal finite free resolution of \)M$ as above; the snake
393	  lemma applied to multiplication by \(x\) gives a minimal finite free resolution
394	  \[
395	    0 \to (R/xR)^{n_e} \to (R/xR)^{n_{e-1}} \to \cdots \to (R/xR)^{n_0} \to M/xM \to 0
396	  \]
397	  of \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\), of the same length \(e\). So
398	  \(\text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\). By Stacks
399	  \texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}, both \(\text{depth}(R/xR) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\) and
400	  \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\), and one verifies
401	  \(\text{depth}_R(M/xM) = \text{depth}_{R/xR}(M/xM)\) (regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}\)
402	  correspond to regular sequences in \(\mathfrak{m}/(x)\)). Apply the inductive hypothesis to
403	  \(M/xM\) over \(R/xR\):
404	  \[
405	    \text{pd}_{R/xR}(M/xM) + \text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(R/xR).
406	  \]
407	  Substituting yields
408	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + (\text{depth}(M) - 1) = \text{depth}(R) - 1\), i.e.\
409	  \(\text{pd}_R(M) + \text{depth}(M) = \text{depth}(R)\), as required.
410	\end{proof}

440	
441	  Inductive-step variant pinning the project-internal helper used by
442	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum}: for a Noetherian local ring
443	  \((R, \mathfrak m)\), a nonzero finite \(R\)-module \(M\) of projective

449	  \uses{lem:depth_drops_by_one}
450	  Replay of the induction step in the main proof's
451	  \emph{inductive step} paragraph (\cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum},
452	  proof block), using \cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one} for the
453	  depth-drop on both \(R\) and \(M\) after quotienting by an
454	  \(M\)-regular \(x \in \mathfrak m\), plus a snake-lemma carving of
455	  the minimal resolution to extract the \(R/(x)\)-minimal resolution
456	  of \(M/xM\) of the same length. The four ingredients are:
457	  \begin{enumerate}
458	    \item Minimal-resolution carving (Stacks
459	      \texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}): every finite free
460	      resolution of a finite module over a Noetherian local ring can
461	      be trimmed to a minimal one of the same projective dimension.
462	    \item ``What is exact'' criterion (Stacks 00MF /
463	      \texttt{proposition-what-exact}): characterises exactness in
464	      terms of depths of \(r\)-minors. Used to extract a common
465	      non-zero-divisor \(x \in \mathfrak m\) for both \(R\) and \(M\).
466	    \item Snake lemma applied to multiplication-by-\(x\) on the
467	      minimal resolution: produces a minimal resolution of \(M/xM\)
468	      over \(R/(x)\) of the same length, so
469	      \(\text{pd}_{R/(x)}(M/xM) = \text{pd}_R(M)\).
470	    \item Depth drops by one
471	      (\cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}): once an \(M\)-regular element
472	      \(x \in \mathfrak m\) is fixed,
473	      \(\text{depth}(M/xM) = \text{depth}(M) - 1\) and likewise on \(R\).
474	  \end{enumerate}
475	  Combining (1)-(4) with the inductive hypothesis on \(M/xM\) over
476	  \(R/(x)\) closes the inductive step. Pieces (1)-(3) remain Mathlib
477	  gaps as of \texttt{b80f227}; piece (4) is now formalised
478	  axiom-clean (\cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}).
479	\end{proof}
480	
481	\begin{lemma}
482	
483	\leanok
484	  [Depth drops by one under a regular element]
485	  \label{lem:depth_drops_by_one}
486	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.depth_quotSMulTop_succ_eq_depth_of_isSMulRegular}
487	  \uses{def:depth, lem:depth_via_ext}
488	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LL (\texttt{lemma-depth-drops-by-one}).}
489	
490	  For a Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak m)\), a nonzero finite
491	  \(R\)-module \(M\), and an \(M\)-regular element \(x \in \mathfrak m\),
492	  \[
493	    \text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M/xM) + 1 \;=\; \text{depth}_{\mathfrak m}(M).
494	  \]
495	\end{lemma}
496	\begin{proof}
497	  Reduces to the Ext characterisation
498	  (\cref{lem:depth_via_ext}) on both sides; the short exact sequence
499	  \(0 \to M \xrightarrow{\,\cdot x\,} M \to M/xM \to 0\) gives a
500	  long exact sequence in \(\text{Ext}^*_R(\kappa, -)\) on which
501	  multiplication by \(x\) is zero (since \(x \in \text{Ann}_R \kappa\)),
502	  breaking the LES into short exact pieces
503	  \(0 \to \text{Ext}^i(\kappa, M) \to \text{Ext}^i(\kappa, M/xM) \to
504	  \text{Ext}^{i+1}(\kappa, M) \to 0\). The translation to the
505	  \(\mathbb N_\infty\) depth equality goes through
506	  \texttt{ENat.forall\_natCast\_le\_iff\_le}.
507	\end{proof}

509	\subsection{Inductive-step substrate: per-gap decomposition of
510	  \texorpdfstring{\(\text{pd}(M) = k+1\)}{pd(M) = k+1}}
511	\label{subsec:succ_pd_gap_sequence}
512	
513	The inductive step
514	\cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}
515	of the Auslander--Buchsbaum formula is gated, as of the current pinned
516	Mathlib snapshot, by exactly three absent substrate ingredients
517	(gaps (1)--(3)) plus a fourth that is now in hand
518	(gap (4), \cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}). This subsection records the
519	decomposition that the proof block of
520	\cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd} folds into four bullets:
521	its purpose is to make the dependency graph and the per-gap effort
522	estimates explicit, so that the substrate gaps can be formalised
523	independently (and where the dependency graph permits, in parallel).
524	
525	\paragraph{Sequencing constraint.} The three open gaps are
526	\emph{not} mutually independent at the substrate level. Gap (3)
527	(snake-lemma carving of the minimal resolution by an
528	\(M\)-regular element \(x \in \mathfrak m\)) needs gap (1)
529	(minimal-resolution normalisation: every finite free resolution of
530	a finite module over a Noetherian local ring trims to a minimal one
531	of the same projective dimension) as input, because the snake-lemma
532	output is only known to be a \emph{minimal} resolution of \(M/xM\)
533	over \(R/(x)\) of the same length when the input resolution is itself
534	minimal. Gap (2) (Stacks 00MF, the ``what is exact'' criterion in
535	terms of depths of \(r\)-minors of the transition matrices) is
536	independent of gaps (1) and (3) at the substrate level: it is a
537	self-contained criterion on a complex of finite free modules and
538	feeds the closure assembly without going through gap (1) or gap (3).
539	
540	\paragraph{Dependency diagram.} Read top-to-bottom as ``provides
541	input to'':
542	\begin{verbatim}
543	                gap (1)
544	       minimal-resolution carving
545	   (Stacks lemma-add-trivial-complex)
546	                  |
547	                  v
548	                gap (3)
549	        snake lemma on minimal
550	        resolution under x in m
551	                  |
552	                  v
553	   +--------------+--------------+
554	   |   L1299 closure assembly    |   <--   gap (2)
555	   |   (succ-pd inductive step)  |        Stacks 00MF
556	   +-----------------------------+        "what is exact"
557	                  ^
558	                  |
559	                gap (4)
560	       depth drops by one (CLOSED;
561	       see lem:depth_drops_by_one)
562	\end{verbatim}
563	Gap (4) is already formalised
564	(\cref{lem:depth_drops_by_one}, the project's
565	\texttt{depth\_quotSMulTop\_succ\_eq\_depth\_of\_isSMulRegular})
566	and feeds the closure assembly directly. Gap (2) feeds the closure
567	assembly independently of the gap (1) \(\to\) gap (3) chain.
568	
569	\paragraph{Per-gap effort estimates.} The following table records,
570	for each gap, the estimated formalisation effort (lines of code in
571	the project's Lean source style), a coarse iter budget, the
572	Mathlib-at-\texttt{b80f227} status, and substrate dependencies on
573	other gaps:
574	\begin{center}
575	  \begin{tabular}{|c|l|c|c|c|c|}
576	    \hline
577	    Gap & Statement                              & LOC est.  & Iters
578	        & Mathlib status     & Depends on \\
579	    \hline
580	    (1) & Minimal-resolution carving             & 40--80    & 1--2
581	        & per-syzygy step CLOSED iter-199
582	                                                                & none \\
583	    (2) & Stacks 00MF (``what is exact'')        & 150--200  & 2--3
584	        & absent             & none \\
585	    (3) & Snake lemma on minimal resolution      & ---       & ---
586	        & \textbf{OBVIATED iter-200} & --- \\
587	    (4) & Depth drops by one                     & ---       & ---
588	        & \textbf{CLOSED}    & --- \\
589	    \hline
590	  \end{tabular}
591	\end{center}
592	The gap (1) estimate is anchored to Bruns--Herzog,
593	\emph{Cohen--Macaulay Rings}, Construction~1.5.18 together with the
594	minimal-resolution induction (\S1.5): the trimming algorithm is a
595	finite-rank linear-algebra computation followed by induction on the
596	length of the resolution. The gap (2) estimate reflects the chain
597	Bruns--Herzog Theorem~1.4.13 \(\to\) Eagon--Northcott
598	\(\to\) Buchsbaum--Eisenbud's exactness criterion (Stacks 00MF /
599	\texttt{proposition-what-exact}), which characterises exactness of a
600	complex of finite free modules in terms of depths of the ideals of
601	\(r\)-minors of the transition matrices. Gap (2) is the largest of
602	the three open substrates and is a natural candidate for an upstream
603	Mathlib contribution rather than a project-internal proof.
604	
605	\paragraph{Gap (3) OBVIATED iter-200.} The iter-200 ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB
606	pivot (\ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}) replaces the literal
607	\(\texttt{ChainComplex}\,\mathbb N\) carrier with the abstract
608	\(\texttt{HasProjectiveDimensionLT}\) SES-descent pattern; under this
609	pattern, minimality of the resolution is \emph{not} used as input
610	anywhere in the inductive step. Consequently the snake-lemma carving
611	of the minimal resolution (gap (3)) is no longer a substrate
612	requirement: only gap (2) (\(\texttt{pd}\,M > 0 \Rightarrow
613	\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\)) remains the binding gap for
614	the closure of \ref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}, and the
615	per-syzygy step of gap (1) is consumed only through the SES at
616	\(0 \to \ker f \to R^n \to M \to 0\) (no minimality input required).
617	See \ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot} for the SES-descent details.
618	
619	\paragraph{Gap (2) Stacks 00MF proof recipe.} The
620	Buchsbaum--Eisenbud exactness criterion (Stacks 00MF /
621	\texttt{proposition-what-exact}) reads: a complex
622	\(\dots \to R^{r_2} \xrightarrow{\varphi_2} R^{r_1}
623	 \xrightarrow{\varphi_1} R^{r_0}\) of finite free modules over a
624	Noetherian local ring \(R\) is exact at \(R^{r_i}\) (for \(i \geq 1\))
625	if and only if \(\texttt{rank}\,\varphi_{i+1} + \texttt{rank}\,\varphi_i
626	= r_i\) and \(\texttt{depth}\,I(\varphi_i) \geq i\), where
627	\(I(\varphi)\) denotes the ideal of \(r\)-minors of \(\varphi\) for
628	\(r = \texttt{rank}\,\varphi\). The recipe for an iter-201+ prover:
629	(a) define \(I(\varphi) \subseteq R\) as the ideal of all
630	\(\texttt{rank}\,\varphi\)-minors of \(\varphi\) (Mathlib supports
631	this via \(\texttt{Matrix.minors}\) and \(\texttt{Ideal.span}\) over the
632	finite index set of \(\binom{n}{r}\) tuples); (b) prove the depth
633	lower bound via induction on \(i\) using a Koszul-complex argument
634	on the regular sequence implicit in the exactness data; (c) derive
635	\(\texttt{pd}\,M > 0 \Rightarrow \texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\)
636	as a corollary by taking the start \(\dots \to R^{r_1} \to R^{r_0} \to
637	M\) of a minimal resolution and applying the criterion at \(i = 1\).
638	Estimated \(\sim 150-200\) LOC project-side; Mathlib upstream PR
639	candidate.
640	
641	\paragraph{Closure assembly (iter-200 ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB pivot).} The
642	iter-200 substrate landing in \ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}
643	replaces the original (1)+(3)-dependent assembly with an SES-descent
644	recipe: given an \(M\) with \(\texttt{pd}_R(M) = k+1\), the
645	per-syzygy step of gap (1)
646	(\ref{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}) supplies a
647	surjection \(f : R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\); the SES
648	\(0 \to \ker f \to R^n \to M \to 0\) plus the iter-200 helpers
649	\(\texttt{hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_ker\_of\_surjection}\) +
650	\(\texttt{hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_succ\_of\_hasProjectiveDimensionLT\_ker}\)
651	give \(\texttt{pd}_R(\ker f) = k\) exactly; gap (2) (the
652	Buchsbaum--Eisenbud criterion at the start of a minimal resolution)
653	delivers \(\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\); the iter-200
654	helper
655	\(\texttt{depth\_ker\_ge\_min\_of\_surjection\_finite\_localRing}\)
656	gives \(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f \geq \min(\texttt{depth}\,R,
657	\texttt{depth}\,M + 1) = \texttt{depth}\,M + 1\); a complementary LES
658	upper bound (or the IH applied to \(\ker f\) under the strict
659	descent of \(\texttt{pd}\)) gives the matching upper bound. Combine
660	to get \(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,M + 1\), then apply
661	the IH (\(\texttt{pd}\,\ker f = k\)) for
662	\(k + \texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,R\), which rearranges
663	to \((k+1) + \texttt{depth}\,M = \texttt{depth}\,R\) as required.
664	Estimated closure-assembly cost (gaps in hand): \(\sim 50-80\) LOC
665	inductive code.
666	
667	\paragraph{Iter budget refinement.} Aggregating the per-gap
668	estimates with the closure assembly: gap (1) at 1--2 iters, gap (2)
669	at 2--3 iters, gap (3) at 1--2 iters, and the closure assembly
670	at 1 iter gives a total remaining iter budget of \textbf{5--8 iters}
671	for \cref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd}. The schedule may
672	parallelise across the gap (2) branch and the gap (1) \(\to\) gap (3)
673	chain --- so wall-clock could be as low as
674	\(\max(\text{gap (2)},\, \text{gap (1)} + \text{gap (3)}) +
675	\text{closure}\), i.e.\ \(\max(2\text{--}3,\, 2\text{--}4) + 1\),
676	yielding 3--5 iters in the parallel-best case. The substrate
677	sequencing constraint (gap (3) requires gap (1)) is hard; the gap
678	(2) branch is genuinely independent of that chain and could be
679	dispatched as a parallel prover lane the moment a free slot opens.
680	This estimate refines the earlier ``~6--12 iters'' coarse budget
681	recorded in \texttt{STRATEGY.md}: the refined window is somewhat
682	tighter on the upper end (5--8 vs.\ 6--12 sequential) and is
683	substantially tighter under parallel dispatch (3--5 parallel-best
684	vs.\ 6--12).

686	\subsection{Gap (1) first-step substrate (iter-199): per-syzygy
687	  minimal surjection from a finite free module}
688	\label{subsec:ab_gap1_first_step}
689	
690	\begin{lemma}
691	
692	\leanok
693	  [Per-syzygy minimal surjection from \(R^n\) for finite modules
694	    over a local ring]
695	  \label{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}
696	  \lean{RingTheory.Module.exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}
697	  \uses{def:depth}
698	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00LK
699	  % (lemma-add-trivial-complex; minimal-resolution carving substrate)
700	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L24590-L24623).
701	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
702	  % "Suppose $R$ is a ring. Let
703	  %     \ldots
704	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i + 1}}
705	  %     R^{n_i}
706	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_i}
707	  %     R^{n_{i-1}}
708	  %     \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i-1}}
709	  %     \ldots
710	  %  be a complex of finite free $R$-modules. Suppose that for some $i$
711	  %  some matrix coefficient of the map $\varphi_i$ is invertible.
712	  %  Then the displayed complex is isomorphic to the direct sum of a
713	  %  complex
714	  %     \ldots \to
715	  %     R^{n_{i + 2}} \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i + 2}}
716	  %     R^{n_{i + 1}} \to
717	  %     R^{n_i - 1} \to
718	  %     R^{n_{i - 1} - 1} \to
719	  %     R^{n_{i - 2}} \xrightarrow{\varphi_{i - 2}}
720	  %     R^{n_{i - 3}} \to
721	  %     \ldots
722	  %  and the complex $\ldots \to 0 \to R \to R \to 0 \to \ldots$ where
723	  %  the map $R \to R$ is the identity map."
724	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00LK
725	    (\texttt{lemma-add-trivial-complex}).}
726	  Let \(R\) be a local ring (Noetherian-ness NOT required at this
727	  step), \(\mathfrak m \subseteq R\) the maximal ideal,
728	  \(\kappa = R/\mathfrak m\) the residue field, and \(M\) a finite
729	  \(R\)-module. Then there exist \(n \in \mathbb N\) and an
730	  \(R\)-linear surjection \(f : R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\) such that:
731	  \begin{enumerate}
732	    \item \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\) (the minimal
733	      generator count of \(M\), via Nakayama);
734	    \item \(\ker f \subseteq \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) (the
735	      ``minimality'' condition: every entry of every relation lives
736	      in the maximal ideal, so the chosen lift has no trivial
737	      summands).
738	  \end{enumerate}
739	\end{lemma}
740	\begin{proof}
741	  Pick a \(\kappa\)-basis
742	  \(b : \mathrm{Fin}\,n \to \kappa \otimes_R M\) via
743	  \texttt{Module.finBasis} with
744	  \(n = \dim_\kappa(\kappa \otimes_R M)\). Surjectivity of the
745	  canonical map \(M \twoheadrightarrow \kappa \otimes_R M\)
746	  (composition of \texttt{TensorProduct.mk\_surjective} and
747	  \texttt{Ideal.Quotient.mk\_surjective}) gives lifts
748	  \(m_i \in M\) with \(1 \otimes m_i = b_i\). Define
749	  \(f : R^n \to M\) by \(f x := \sum_i x_i \cdot m_i\) via
750	  \texttt{(Pi.basisFun R (Fin n)).constr R m}.
751	
752	  Surjectivity: \(\mathrm{range}\, f = \mathrm{span}_R(m_i)\) by
753	  \texttt{Module.Basis.constr\_range}, and
754	  \(\mathrm{span}_R(m_i) = M\) by Nakayama via
755	  \texttt{IsLocalRing.span\_eq\_top\_of\_tmul\_eq\_basis}.
756	
757	  Kernel containment: for \(x \in \ker f\) the relation
758	  \(\sum_i x_i m_i = 0\) tensors to
759	  \(\sum_i (1 \otimes x_i \cdot m_i) = 0\); using
760	  \texttt{TensorProduct.tmul\_smul} and the linear independence of
761	  \(b\) (\texttt{Module.Basis.linearIndependent}) we obtain
762	  \(\mathrm{residue}_R(x_i) = 0\) for every \(i\), i.e.\
763	  \(x_i \in \mathfrak m\). Decomposing
764	  \(x = \sum_i x_i \cdot \mathrm{Pi.single}\,i\,1\) then yields
765	  \(x \in \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\) by
766	  \texttt{Submodule.smul\_mem\_smul} and
767	  \texttt{Submodule.sum\_mem}.
768	\end{proof}
769	
770	This lemma is the \emph{per-syzygy} step of gap (1)'s
771	minimal-resolution carving: given a finite \(R\)-module \(M\)
772	over a local ring \(R\), it produces a minimal surjection
773	\(R^n \twoheadrightarrow M\). Iter-200's ALIGN\_WITH\_MATHLIB pivot
774	(\ref{subsec:ab_gap1_haspdlt_pivot}) replaces the original
775	\(\mathbb N\)-recursive \(\texttt{ChainComplex}\) assembly target
776	with abstract \(\texttt{HasProjectiveDimensionLT}\) SES descent: only
777	the per-syzygy SES is consumed; the iterated chain-complex carrier
778	is not needed.
779

882	give \(\texttt{pd}\,\ker f = k\) exactly;
883	\ref{lem:depth_ker_ge_min_of_surjection_finite_localRing} plus gap (2)
884	plus a complementary LES upper bound (or the IH) give

892	\paragraph{Iter-201 Path B closure recipe (LES-injectivity +
893	matrix-collapse).} The iter-201 \texttt{ab-stacks00mf} mathlib-
894	analogist verdict identifies an alternative closure that sidesteps
895	gap (2): instead of building Stacks 00MF as a standalone substrate,
896	split \ref{lem:auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd} into an
897	\emph{inductive step} (\(\texttt{pd}\,M = k+1, k \geq 1\)) and a
898	\emph{base case} (\(\texttt{pd}\,M = 1\)). Each piece uses only
899	already-existing LES infrastructure plus one new
900	\(\sim 50\)--\(80\) LOC matrix-collapse helper.
901	
902	\textit{Inductive step \(\texttt{pd}\,M = k+1, k \geq 1\)
903	\((\sim 50\)--\(80\) LOC).} From
904	\ref{lem:hasProjectiveDimensionLT_ker_of_surjection} +
905	the contrapositive of
906	\ref{lem:hasProjectiveDimensionLT_succ_of_hasProjectiveDimensionLT_ker},
907	\(\texttt{pd}\,\ker f = k\) exactly. Strong induction gives
908	\(k + \texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,R\), hence
909	\(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f < \texttt{depth}\,R\) since \(k \geq 1\).
910	Lower bound \(\texttt{depth}\,M \geq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f - 1\):
911	\(\texttt{depth\_of\_short\_exact}\) part (2). Upper bound
912	\(\texttt{depth}\,M \leq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f - 1\): by
913	contradiction --- assume \(\texttt{depth}\,M \geq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f\),
914	look at the LES of \(\texttt{Ext}^*(\kappa, -)\) at
915	\(i = \texttt{depth}\,\ker f\), infer
916	\(\texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,\ker f}(\kappa, R^n) \neq 0\)
917	(forcing \(\texttt{depth}\,R \leq \texttt{depth}\,\ker f\)),
918	contradicting the IH.
919	
920	\textit{Base case \(\texttt{pd}\,M = 1\) \((\sim 80\)--\(120\) LOC).}
921	\(\ker f\) is projective (\(\texttt{pd}\,\ker f = 0\)), hence free
922	over the local Noetherian \(R\) by Mathlib's
923	\(\texttt{Module.free\_of\_flat\_of\_isLocalRing}\) +
924	\(\texttt{Module.Flat.of\_projective}\). So \(\ker f \cong R^m\) with
925	\(m \geq 1\). The inclusion \(\ker f \hookrightarrow R^n\) is an
926	\((n \times m)\)-matrix \(A\) with entries in
927	\(\texttt{Ann}(\kappa) = \mathfrak m\) (from
928	\ref{lem:exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing}'s minimality
929	clause \(\ker f \subseteq \mathfrak m \cdot R^n\)).
930	
931	The new \textbf{matrix-collapse helper}
932	(\(\sim 50\)--\(80\) LOC, target site
933	\texttt{AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} L1290 area):
934	\[
935	  \forall i \geq 0,\quad
936	  (- \circ \texttt{Ext.mk}_0\,A) \colon
937	  \texttt{Ext}^i(\kappa, R^m) \longrightarrow
938	  \texttt{Ext}^i(\kappa, R^n)
939	  \quad \text{is the zero map}.
940	\]
941	Proof: \(A = \sum_{i,j} A_{ij} \cdot E_{ij}\) (basis-matrix sum); by
942	Mathlib's \(\texttt{Ext.add\_comp}\) +
943	\(\texttt{Ext.smul\_comp}\) +
944	\(\texttt{Ext.bilinearCompOfLinear}\) (from
945	\texttt{Mathlib/Algebra/Homology/DerivedCategory/Ext/Linear.lean}),
946	the postcomp distributes. Each summand has a scalar factor
947	\(A_{ij} \in \texttt{Ann}(\kappa)\) acting on
948	\(\texttt{Ext}^i(\kappa, R^n)\), killed by the existing
949	\(\texttt{ext\_smul\_eq\_zero\_of\_mem\_annihilator}\) (L229). Sum
950	collapses to zero.
951	
952	With matrix-collapse, the LES at \(i = \texttt{depth}\,R - 1\)
953	gives
954	\[
955	  \texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R - 1}(\kappa, M)
956	  \xrightarrow{\;\delta\;}
957	  \texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, \ker f)
958	  \xrightarrow{\;(- \circ A)\;}
959	  \texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, R^n)
960	\]
961	with the second arrow zero. If
962	\(\texttt{depth}\,M \geq \texttt{depth}\,R\), the source is zero,
963	hence \(\texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, \ker f) = 0\) ---
964	but \(\texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, \ker f) \simeq
965	\texttt{Ext}^{\texttt{depth}\,R}(\kappa, R^m) \neq 0\)
966	(\(m \geq 1\), \(\texttt{depth}\,R^m = \texttt{depth}\,R\)).
967	Contradiction. So \(\texttt{depth}\,M < \texttt{depth}\,R\), and the
968	base case
969	\(\texttt{pd}\,M + \texttt{depth}\,M = \texttt{depth}\,R\) follows
970	from \(\texttt{depth}\,\ker f = \texttt{depth}\,R\) (since \(\ker f\)
971	free).
972	
973	\textbf{Total Path B cost: \(\sim 130\)--\(200\) LOC} comparable to
974	Path A but with better composition --- every new lemma reuses
975	existing axiom-clean primitives, and the matrix-collapse helper is
976	independently useful for any future ``minimal matrix annihilates

980	\section{Corollary: regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay}
981	\label{sec:ab_regular_cm}
982	
983	\begin{definition}
984	
985	\leanok
986	  [Cohen--Macaulay local ring]
987	  \label{def:cohen_macaulay_local}
988	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay}
989	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM)
990	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25288-L25299).
991	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
992	  % "A Noetherian local ring \(R\) is called {\it Cohen-Macaulay}
993	  %  if it is Cohen-Macaulay as a module over itself."
994	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00N4 (definition-local-ring-CM).}
995	  A Noetherian local ring \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is called \emph{Cohen--Macaulay} if
996	  \(\text{depth}(R) = \dim(R)\) (equivalently, if \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay as a module
997	  over itself, where Cohen--Macaulay for a finite \(R\)-module \(M\) means
998	  \(\dim(\text{Supp}(M)) = \text{depth}(M)\), cf.\ Stacks tag 00N3).
999	\end{definition}
1000	
1001	% NOTE (iter-185 review, supersedes iter-184): the Lean assembly
1002	% `CohenMacaulay.of_regular` calls a non-private helper
1003	% `RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` (AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean
1004	% L1096 — line drifted from iter-184's L944 reference) that now carries a
1005	% structural induction scaffold via `regularLocal_inductive_step`, with the
1006	% load-bearing sorry pushed into two named helpers: (i)
1007	% `exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ` consolidating the
1008	% Stacks 00NQ + 00NU substrate gaps in one signature, and (ii) an inline
1009	% technical-bridge sorry inside `regularLocal_inductive_step` for the
1010	% `R⧸(x)`-vs-`QuotSMulTop x R` linear-equiv upgrade (~10-20 LOC iter-186 close
1011	% via `Submodule.ideal_span_singleton_smul` + `QuotSMulTop.mem_annihilator`).
1012	% Neither has a `\lean{...}` pin in this chapter (the lean-vs-blueprint-checker
1013	% iter185-auslander minor flagged adding a pin for the public-facing
1014	% `exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` itself; iter-186+ chapter touch can add

1017	% (~300 LOC); (b) Koszul-homology bypass via `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index`;

1019	\begin{corollary}
1020	
1021	\leanok
1022	  [Regular local rings are Cohen--Macaulay]
1023	  \label{cor:regular_cohen_macaulay}
1024	  \lean{RingTheory.CohenMacaulay.of_regular}
1025	  
1026	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM)
1027	  % (read from references/stacks-algebra.tex, L25714-L25730).
1028	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1029	  % "Let \(R\) be a regular local ring and let
1030	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) be a minimal set of generators
1031	  %  for the maximal ideal \(\mathfrak m\). Then
1032	  %  \(x_1, \ldots, x_d\) is a regular sequence, and
1033	  %  each \(R/(x_1, \ldots, x_c)\) is a regular local ring
1034	  %  of dimension \(d - c\). In particular \(R\) is Cohen-Macaulay."
1035	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 00OD (lemma-regular-ring-CM).}
1036	  Every regular Noetherian local ring is Cohen--Macaulay: if \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) is
1037	  regular local of Krull dimension \(d\), then \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\).
1038	\end{corollary}
1039	
1040	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1041	% "Note that \(R/x_1R\) is a Noetherian local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\)
1042	%  by Lemma REF with \(x_2, \ldots, x_d\)
1043	%  generating the maximal ideal. Hence it is a regular local ring by definition.
1044	%  Since \(R\) is a domain by Lemma REF
1045	%  \(x_1\) is a nonzerodivisor."
1046	\begin{proof}
1047	  
1048	  
1049	  
1050	  
1051	  Let \((R, \mathfrak{m})\) be regular of dimension \(d\) and pick a minimal generating set
1052	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) of \(\mathfrak{m}\). By Stacks tag 00NQ (lemma-regular-domain), \(R\) is
1053	  an integral domain, so \(x_1\) is a non-zero-divisor. The quotient \(R/x_1 R\) is a Noetherian
1054	  local ring of dimension \(\geq d - 1\) (by Krull's principal ideal theorem,
1055	  \texttt{lemma-one-equation}) with maximal ideal generated by \(x_2, \dots, x_d\), hence
1056	  has dimension exactly \(d - 1\) and is again regular of dimension \(d - 1\). Inducting on \(d\),
1057	  \(x_1, \dots, x_d\) is an \(R\)-regular sequence, so
1058	  \(\text{depth}(R) \geq d\). The reverse inequality \(\text{depth}(R) \leq \dim(R) = d\)
1059	  always holds for any nonzero finite module over a Noetherian local ring
1060	  (Stacks tag 00LK, \texttt{lemma-bound-depth}). Thus \(\text{depth}(R) = d = \dim(R)\), i.e.\
1061	  \(R\) is Cohen--Macaulay.
1062	
1063	  \emph{Remark on the Auslander--Buchsbaum route.} The same conclusion can be obtained from
1064	  \cref{thm:auslander_buchsbaum} as follows: take \(M := R\) itself. Then \(M\) is
1065	  finitely generated and projective (free of rank \(1\)), so \(\text{pd}_R(R) = 0\). The
1066	  formula gives \(\text{depth}(R) = 0 + \text{depth}(R)\), a tautology. The non-tautological
1067	  use of Auslander--Buchsbaum is rather: for a regular local ring of dimension \(d\), every
1068	  finitely generated module \(M\) has \(\text{pd}_R(M) \leq d\) (Stacks tag 00O2,
1069	  \texttt{proposition-finite-gl-dim-regular}); applying the formula to such an \(M\) then
1070	  relates \(\text{depth}(M)\) to \(d - \text{pd}_R(M)\), which is the form most often used in
1071	  practice. The direct regular-sequence argument above is the cleanest proof of
1072	  \(R\) Cohen--Macaulay itself.
1073	\end{proof}
1074	
1075	\section{Application: codim-1 extension on a regular surface (gating A.4.a)}
1076	\label{sec:ab_application_to_a4a}
1077	
1078	The downstream consumer is A.4.a: extending a rational map
1079	\(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) from a regular projective surface \(S\) to an abelian variety \(A\)
1080	across a closed point \(x \in S\) that is missing from the domain of definition \(U \subseteq S\).
1081	The classical mechanism (cf.\ Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, III.7) is:
1082	
1083	\begin{enumerate}
1084	  \item The local ring \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\) is a regular Noetherian local ring of Krull
1085	    dimension \(2\) (because \(S\) is regular and \(\dim S = 2\)).
1086	  \item By Corollary~REF it is Cohen--Macaulay, so
1087	    \(\text{depth}(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}) = 2\). Equivalently, the maximal ideal contains an
1088	    \(\mathcal{O}_{S,x}\)-regular sequence of length \(2\) (any two transverse generators).
1089	  \item Sections of the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_S\) on \(U = S \setminus \{x\}\) extend
1090	    uniquely across \(x\) because \(H^0_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) and
1091	    \(H^1_x(\mathcal{O}_S) = 0\) (these local-cohomology vanishings are equivalent to depth
1092	    \(\geq 2\) at \(x\) by Stacks tag 0AVF; see Hartshorne, III.3.5, for the surface case).
1093	  \item For a rational map \(f : S \dashrightarrow A\) with codimension-\(\geq 2\) indeterminacy
1094	    locus on a regular surface, the same depth-\(\geq 2\) input produces a sheaf-theoretic
1095	    extension of \(f\) to all of \(S\): pull back any sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_A\)-affine-locally,
1096	    obtain sections over \(U\), and extend across \(x\) by the above.
1097	\end{enumerate}
1098	
1099	So Corollary~REF is the gating input: the geometric A.4.a
1100	proof can be written conditional on the algebraic Auslander--Buchsbaum corollary, and the
1101	two halves develop independently --- as long as Theorem~REF and
1102	Corollary~REF are formalized in
1103	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/AuslanderBuchsbaum.lean} with the signatures pinned
1104	in this chapter, A.4.a can consume them as Mathlib-style imports.
1105

932	(for the SMul-regular conjunct) plus `quotSMulTopPiConstLinearEquiv` +
933	`LinearEquiv.isRegular_congr` (to bridge the quotient regularity to the IH on

935	private lemma isRegular_pi_const_iff_of_nonempty
936	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] [DecidableEq ι] [Nonempty ι]
937	    (rs : List R) :
938	    ∀ {M : Type v} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M],
939	      RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular (ι → M) rs ↔
940	        RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular M rs := by
941	  induction rs with
942	  | nil =>
943	    intro M _ _
944	    refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩
945	    · rintro ⟨_, hPi_top⟩
946	      refine ⟨.nil R M, ?_⟩
947	      rw [Ideal.ofList_nil, Submodule.bot_smul] at hPi_top ⊢
948	      intro habs
949	      apply hPi_top
950	      rw [Submodule.eq_bot_iff] at habs ⊢
951	      intro f _
952	      funext i
953	      exact habs (f i) trivial
954	    · rintro ⟨_, hM_top⟩
955	      refine ⟨.nil R (ι → M), ?_⟩
956	      rw [Ideal.ofList_nil, Submodule.bot_smul] at hM_top ⊢
957	      obtain ⟨j⟩ := ‹Nonempty ι›
958	      intro habs
959	      apply hM_top
960	      rw [Submodule.eq_bot_iff] at habs ⊢
961	      intro m _
962	      have hsingle : (Pi.single j m : ι → M) = 0 := habs _ trivial
963	      have heval := congr_fun hsingle j
964	      rwa [Pi.single_eq_same, Pi.zero_apply] at heval
965	  | cons r rs' ih =>
966	    intro M _ _
967	    rw [RingTheory.Sequence.isRegular_cons_iff, RingTheory.Sequence.isRegular_cons_iff]
968	    refine and_congr ?_ ?_
969	    · constructor
970	      · intro h
971	        obtain ⟨j⟩ := ‹Nonempty ι›

975	    · rw [LinearEquiv.isRegular_congr

979	/-- **Main iter-194 Lane G closure.** For any commutative ring `R`, ideal `I`,
980	`R`-module `M`, and nonempty finite type `ι`, the depth of the Pi module
981	`ι → M` equals the depth of `M`:
982	```
983	  depth I (ι → M) = depth I M.
984	```
985	This is the substrate for the `pd_R(M) = 0` case of the Auslander–Buchsbaum
986	formula: a finite free module `M ≃ₗ[R] Fin k → R` has `depth(M) = depth(R)`,
987	so `0 + depth(M) = depth(R)` holds. -/
988	lemma depth_pi_const_eq_depth_of_nonempty
989	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] (I : Ideal R)
990	    {ι : Type*} [Fintype ι] [DecidableEq ι] [Nonempty ι]
991	    {M : Type v} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] :
992	    depth I (ι → M) = depth I M := by
993	  unfold depth
994	  by_cases h : I • (⊤ : Submodule R (ι → M)) = ⊤
995	  · rw [if_pos h, if_pos ((ideal_smul_top_pi_const_eq_top_iff I).mp h)]
996	  · rw [if_neg h, if_neg (mt (ideal_smul_top_pi_const_eq_top_iff I).mpr h)]
997	    congr 1
998	    ext n

1005	/-! ### Helper iter-198 Lane AB (axiom-clean): depth drops by exactly one for a regular
1006	element (Stacks `lemma-depth-drops-by-one`).
1007	
1008	For a Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)`, a nonzero finite `R`-module `M`, and `x ∈ 𝔪` an
1009	`M`-regular element, the depth of `M/xM` is exactly one less than the depth of `M`:
1010	`depth(M/xM) + 1 = depth(M)`.
1011	
1012	This is one of the four substrate gaps for the Auslander–Buchsbaum n=k+1 step (the
1013	"depth-drops-by-one" piece). The proof packages the long-exact sequence of
1014	`Ext^*(κ, -)` on the SES `0 → M →[x] M → M/xM → 0`, using that
1015	`[x]` is zero on every `Ext^*(κ, M)` (since `x ∈ Ann κ`) to break the LES into short
1016	exact pieces `0 → Ext^i(κ, M) → Ext^i(κ, M/xM) → Ext^{i+1}(κ, M) → 0`.
1017	
1018	Routed through `depth_eq_smallest_ext_index` on both sides + the `ENat.forall_natCast_le_iff_le`

1020	lemma depth_quotSMulTop_succ_eq_depth_of_isSMulRegular
1021	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
1022	    {M : Type u} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [_root_.Module.Finite R M]
1023	    [Nontrivial M] {x : R} (hxMem : x ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R)
1024	    (hxReg : IsSMulRegular M x) :
1025	    depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) (QuotSMulTop x M) + 1 =
1026	      depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M := by
1027	  haveI hMxM_nt : Nontrivial (QuotSMulTop x M) :=
1028	    nontrivial_quotSMulTop_of_mem_maximalIdeal M hxMem
1029	  haveI : _root_.Module.Finite R (QuotSMulTop x M) := inferInstance
1030	  -- κ as a ModuleCat for compact notation.
1031	  set κ : ModuleCat.{u} R := ModuleCat.of R (IsLocalRing.ResidueField R) with hκ
1032	  -- x annihilates κ.
1033	  have hxAnnih : x ∈ _root_.Module.annihilator R (IsLocalRing.ResidueField R) := by
1034	    have h : _root_.Module.annihilator R (IsLocalRing.ResidueField R)
1035	        = IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R := Ideal.annihilator_quotient
1036	    rw [h]; exact hxMem
1037	  -- The SES `0 → M →[x] M → M/xM → 0`.
1038	  let S : ShortComplex (ModuleCat.{u} R) :=
1039	    ModuleCat.smulShortComplex (ModuleCat.of R M) x
1040	  have hS : S.ShortExact := hxReg.smulShortComplex_shortExact
1041	  have hSf_eq_smul : S.f = x • (𝟙 (ModuleCat.of R M) : _ ⟶ _) := rfl
1042	  -- `[x]_*` is zero on every `Ext^i(κ, M)` via the helper `ext_smul_eq_zero_of_mem_annihilator`.
1043	  have hSf_kill : ∀ {i : ℕ} (e : Abelian.Ext.{u} κ (ModuleCat.of R M) i),
1044	      e.comp (CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.f) (add_zero i) = 0 := by
1045	    intro i e
1046	    have hcomp :
1047	        e.comp (CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.f) (add_zero i) = x • e := by
1048	      rw [hSf_eq_smul]
1049	      exact (CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.smul_eq_comp_mk₀ e x).symm
1050	    rw [hcomp]
1051	    exact ext_smul_eq_zero_of_mem_annihilator e hxAnnih
1052	  refine le_antisymm ?_ ?_
1053	  · -- (≥): depth(M/xM) + 1 ≤ depth M.
1054	    rw [← ENat.forall_natCast_le_iff_le]
1055	    intro a ha
1056	    rcases Nat.eq_zero_or_pos a with h0 | hpos
1057	    · subst h0; exact bot_le
1058	    · obtain ⟨n, rfl⟩ := Nat.exists_eq_succ_of_ne_zero (Nat.pos_iff_ne_zero.mp hpos)
1059	      have hcast : ((n + 1 : ℕ) : ℕ∞) = (n : ℕ∞) + 1 := by push_cast; ring
1060	      have hcast' : (Nat.succ n : ℕ) = n + 1 := rfl
1061	      rw [hcast'] at ha
1062	      rw [hcast] at ha
1063	      have hone_ne_top : (1 : ℕ∞) ≠ ⊤ := by simp
1064	      have hn_le_MxM :
1065	          (n : ℕ∞) ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) (QuotSMulTop x M) :=
1066	        (ENat.add_le_add_iff_right hone_ne_top).mp ha
1067	      have hMxM_vanish : ∀ j : ℕ, j < n → ∀ e : Abelian.Ext.{u} κ
1068	            (ModuleCat.of R (QuotSMulTop x M)) j, e = 0 :=
1069	        (depth_eq_smallest_ext_index (M := QuotSMulTop x M) n).mp hn_le_MxM
1070	      change ((n.succ : ℕ) : ℕ∞) ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M
1071	      rw [hcast']
1072	      rw [depth_eq_smallest_ext_index]
1073	      intro i hi e
1074	      rcases Nat.eq_zero_or_pos i with hi0 | hi_pos
1075	      · subst hi0
1076	        -- i = 0 case: x being M-regular ⟹ S.f mono ⟹ postcomp_mk₀_injective_of_mono.
1077	        haveI hmono : CategoryTheory.Mono S.f := by
1078	          rw [ModuleCat.mono_iff_injective]
1079	          exact hxReg
1080	        have hinj := CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.postcomp_mk₀_injective_of_mono κ S.f
1081	        apply hinj
1082	        change e.comp (CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.f) (add_zero 0)
1083	          = (0 : Abelian.Ext.{u} κ (ModuleCat.of R M) 0).comp
1084	              (CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀ S.f) (add_zero 0)
1085	        rw [CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.zero_comp]
1086	        exact hSf_kill e
1087	      · -- i ≥ 1: write i = j + 1, use `covariant_sequence_exact₁`.
1088	        obtain ⟨j, rfl⟩ := Nat.exists_eq_succ_of_ne_zero
1089	          (Nat.pos_iff_ne_zero.mp hi_pos)
1090	        have hj : j < n := by omega
1091	        obtain ⟨x₃, hx₃⟩ :=
1092	          CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₁ κ hS e (hSf_kill e) rfl
1093	        have hx₃_zero : x₃ = 0 := hMxM_vanish j hj _
1094	        rw [hx₃_zero] at hx₃
1095	        simpa using hx₃.symm
1096	  · -- (≤): depth M ≤ depth(M/xM) + 1.
1097	    rw [← ENat.forall_natCast_le_iff_le]
1098	    intro a ha
1099	    rcases Nat.eq_zero_or_pos a with h0 | hpos
1100	    · subst h0; exact bot_le
1101	    · obtain ⟨m, rfl⟩ := Nat.exists_eq_succ_of_ne_zero (Nat.pos_iff_ne_zero.mp hpos)
1102	      have hcast' : (Nat.succ m : ℕ) = m + 1 := rfl
1103	      rw [hcast'] at ha
1104	      have hM_vanish : ∀ i : ℕ, i < m + 1 → ∀ e : Abelian.Ext.{u} κ
1105	            (ModuleCat.of R M) i, e = 0 :=
1106	        (depth_eq_smallest_ext_index (M := M) (m + 1)).mp ha
1107	      have hcast : ((m + 1 : ℕ) : ℕ∞) = (m : ℕ∞) + 1 := by push_cast; ring
1108	      change ((m.succ : ℕ) : ℕ∞) ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) (QuotSMulTop x M) + 1
1109	      rw [hcast', hcast]
1110	      -- It suffices to show m ≤ depth(M/xM).
1111	      have hMxM_bound :
1112	          (m : ℕ∞) ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) (QuotSMulTop x M) := by
1113	        rw [depth_eq_smallest_ext_index]
1114	        intro j hj e
1115	        -- Use `covariant_sequence_exact₃` at n₀ = j, n₁ = j + 1.
1116	        have he_extClass : e.comp hS.extClass (rfl : j + 1 = j + 1) = 0 :=
1117	          hM_vanish (j + 1) (by omega) _
1118	        obtain ⟨x₂, hx₂⟩ :=
1119	          CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.covariant_sequence_exact₃ κ hS e rfl he_extClass
1120	        have hx₂_zero : x₂ = 0 := hM_vanish j (by omega) _
1121	        rw [hx₂_zero] at hx₂
1122	        simpa using hx₂.symm
1123	      gcongr

1125	/-! ### Helper iter-198 Lane AB (axiom-clean): regular-element existence from positive depth.
1126	
1127	For a Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` and a nonzero finite `R`-module `M`, the assertion
1128	`1 ≤ depth_𝔪(M)` is equivalent to the existence of an `M`-regular element `x ∈ 𝔪`. This
1129	is a direct consequence of unfolding the depth definition to a regular-sequence supremum
1130	(Nakayama rules out the `𝔪 • ⊤ = ⊤` trivialisation in the `Nontrivial M` setting) and
1131	reading off a length-1 witness.
1132	
1133	This is the substrate companion to `depth_quotSMulTop_succ_eq_depth_of_isSMulRegular`: the
1134	two together implement the Stacks tag 00LE step "depth-drops-by-one for any regular
1135	element" plus its prerequisite "the regular element exists". -/
1136	lemma exists_isSMulRegular_of_one_le_depth
1137	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
1138	    {M : Type u} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [_root_.Module.Finite R M]
1139	    [Nontrivial M]
1140	    (h : (1 : ℕ∞) ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M) :
1141	    ∃ x : R, x ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R ∧ IsSMulRegular M x := by
1142	  -- Unfold depth: `𝔪 • ⊤ ≠ ⊤` by Nakayama in the `Nontrivial M` finite setting.
1143	  have hne_M : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) • (⊤ : Submodule R M) ≠ ⊤ :=
1144	    Ne.symm (Submodule.top_ne_ideal_smul_of_le_jacobson_annihilator
1145	      (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal_le_jacobson _))
1146	  have hdepth_unfold :
1147	      depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M
1148	        = sSup { k : ℕ∞ | ∃ rs : List R, (rs.length : ℕ∞) = k ∧
1149	          (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R)
1150	          ∧ RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular M rs } := by
1151	    rw [depth, if_neg hne_M]
1152	  rw [hdepth_unfold] at h
1153	  have hlt : (0 : ℕ∞) < sSup { k : ℕ∞ | ∃ rs : List R, (rs.length : ℕ∞) = k ∧
1154	      (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R)
1155	      ∧ RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular M rs } := by
1156	    calc (0 : ℕ∞) < 1 := by norm_num
1157	      _ ≤ _ := h
1158	  obtain ⟨k, ⟨rs, hrs_len, hrs_mem, hrs_reg⟩, hk_lt⟩ := lt_sSup_iff.mp hlt
1159	  -- rs has positive length (since (0 : ℕ∞) < ↑rs.length).
1160	  have hrs_pos : 0 < rs.length := by
1161	    by_contra h_neg
1162	    push Not at h_neg
1163	    have hk0 : rs.length = 0 := Nat.le_zero.mp h_neg
1164	    rw [hk0] at hrs_len
1165	    simp only [Nat.cast_zero] at hrs_len
1166	    rw [← hrs_len] at hk_lt
1167	    exact absurd hk_lt (lt_irrefl _)
1168	  rcases rs with _ | ⟨x, rs_tail⟩
1169	  · simp at hrs_pos
1170	  · refine ⟨x, hrs_mem x List.mem_cons_self, ?_⟩
1171	    exact ((RingTheory.Sequence.isRegular_cons_iff M x rs_tail).mp hrs_reg).1

1173	/-! ### Helper iter-199 Lane AB-gap1 (axiom-clean): minimal surjection substrate.
1174	
1175	For a finite `R`-module `M` over a local ring `R`, there exists a surjective
1176	`R`-linear map `f : (Fin n → R) →ₗ[R] M` of the **minimal possible rank**
1177	`n = dim_κ (κ ⊗_R M)` (where `κ = R/𝔪` is the residue field) whose **kernel
1178	is contained in `𝔪 • ⊤`**. This is the first step of constructing a *minimal
1179	finite free resolution*: iterating the construction on the kernel (which is
1180	itself finitely generated when `R` is Noetherian) produces successive
1181	syzygies whose differential maps each have image in `𝔪` times their target.
1182	
1183	This is the first substrate piece of gap (1) (Stacks
1184	`lemma-add-trivial-complex`) in the Auslander–Buchsbaum closure chain
1185	(`auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd`). It packages the basic
1186	**Nakayama-lift** of a κ-basis of `κ ⊗_R M` to an `R`-spanning family in `M`
1187	and reads off the kernel-containment from linear independence of the basis
1188	combined with the `1 ⊗_R -` evaluation.
1189	
1190	Mathlib substrate used (all axiom-clean):
1191	* `IsLocalRing.span_eq_top_of_tmul_eq_basis` — Nakayama lift of a κ-basis.
1192	* `TensorProduct.mk_surjective` — the `1 ⊗_R -` map is surjective for the
1193	  residue-field tensor.
1194	* `Module.Basis.constr_range` — range of the linear extension equals span of
1195	  the chosen image set.
1196	* `Module.Basis.linearIndependent` — independence of a κ-basis.
1197	* `IsLocalRing.residue_eq_zero_iff` — `r ∈ 𝔪 ↔ residue r = 0`. -/
1198	lemma exists_minimalSurjection_finite_localRing
1199	    (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R]
1200	    (M : Type u) [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [_root_.Module.Finite R M] :
1201	    ∃ (n : ℕ) (f : (Fin n → R) →ₗ[R] M),
1202	      Function.Surjective f ∧
1203	      n = _root_.Module.finrank (IsLocalRing.ResidueField R)
1204	        (TensorProduct R (IsLocalRing.ResidueField R) M) ∧
1205	      LinearMap.ker f ≤ (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) • ⊤ := by
1206	  set κ := IsLocalRing.ResidueField R with hκ
1207	  set n := _root_.Module.finrank κ (TensorProduct R κ M) with hn
1208	  -- Pick a κ-basis of `κ ⊗_R M`.
1209	  let b : _root_.Module.Basis (Fin n) κ (TensorProduct R κ M) :=
1210	    _root_.Module.finBasis κ (TensorProduct R κ M)
1211	  -- The canonical map `(1 : κ) ⊗_R -` is surjective.
1212	  have hsurj_mk : Function.Surjective ((TensorProduct.mk R κ M) 1) := by
1213	    apply TensorProduct.mk_surjective
1214	    exact Ideal.Quotient.mk_surjective
1215	  -- Lift each basis element to a representative in M.
1216	  choose lift hlift using hsurj_mk
1217	  let m : Fin n → M := fun i => lift (b i)
1218	  have hm : ∀ i, (1 : κ) ⊗ₜ[R] m i = b i := fun i => hlift (b i)
1219	  -- Define `f` by sending each standard basis vector of `Fin n → R` to `m i`.
1220	  let f : (Fin n → R) →ₗ[R] M := (Pi.basisFun R (Fin n)).constr R m
1221	  -- Evaluation: `f x = Σ x i • m i`.
1222	  have hf_eval : ∀ x : Fin n → R, f x = ∑ i, x i • m i := by
1223	    intro x
1224	    rw [show f x = ((Pi.basisFun R (Fin n)).constr R) m x from rfl,
1225	        _root_.Module.Basis.constr_apply]
1226	    have h : (Pi.basisFun R (Fin n)).repr x = Finsupp.equivFunOnFinite.symm x := by
1227	      ext i; rw [Pi.basisFun_repr]; rfl
1228	    rw [h, Finsupp.sum_fintype _ _ (by intros; simp)]
1229	    exact Finset.sum_congr rfl (fun i _ => by simp)
1230	  -- Range = span of `m`.
1231	  have hf_range : LinearMap.range f = Submodule.span R (Set.range m) :=
1232	    _root_.Module.Basis.constr_range _ _
1233	  -- Nakayama: span of `m i` equals all of `M`.
1234	  have hspan : Submodule.span R (Set.range m) = ⊤ :=
1235	    IsLocalRing.span_eq_top_of_tmul_eq_basis m b hm
1236	  refine ⟨n, f, ?_, rfl, ?_⟩
1237	  · exact LinearMap.range_eq_top.mp (by rw [hf_range, hspan])
1238	  · -- Kernel containment in `𝔪 • ⊤`.
1239	    intro x hx
1240	    have hfx : f x = 0 := hx
1241	    rw [hf_eval] at hfx
1242	    -- Apply `(1 : κ) ⊗_R -` to `Σ x i • m i = 0`.
1243	    have h1 : (1 : κ) ⊗ₜ[R] (∑ i, x i • m i) = (0 : TensorProduct R κ M) := by
1244	      rw [hfx]; exact TensorProduct.tmul_zero _ _
1245	    rw [TensorProduct.tmul_sum] at h1
1246	    -- Rewrite each summand: `1 ⊗_R (x i • m i) = residue(x i) • b i`.
1247	    have hrewrite : ∀ i, (1 : κ) ⊗ₜ[R] (x i • m i)
1248	        = (IsLocalRing.residue R (x i) : κ) • b i := by
1249	      intro i
1250	      rw [show ((1 : κ) ⊗ₜ[R] (x i • m i))
1251	          = x i • ((1 : κ) ⊗ₜ[R] m i) from
1252	        (TensorProduct.tmul_smul (R := R) (x i) (1 : κ) (m i))]
1253	      rw [hm i]; rfl
1254	    rw [show (∑ i, (1 : κ) ⊗ₜ[R] (x i • m i))
1255	        = ∑ i, (IsLocalRing.residue R (x i) : κ) • b i from
1256	      Finset.sum_congr rfl (fun i _ => hrewrite i)] at h1
1257	    -- Linear independence of `b` forces each `residue (x i) = 0`.
1258	    have hlin : LinearIndependent κ b := b.linearIndependent
1259	    have hall : ∀ i, (IsLocalRing.residue R (x i) : κ) = 0 := by
1260	      have := Fintype.linearIndependent_iff.mp hlin
1261	        (fun i => IsLocalRing.residue R (x i)) h1
1262	      exact fun i => this i
1263	    -- Convert each component-in-𝔪 to `x ∈ 𝔪 • ⊤` via `Pi.single` decomposition.
1264	    have hx_pi : ∀ i, x i ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R := by
1265	      intro i
1266	      have : IsLocalRing.residue R (x i) = 0 := hall i
1267	      rwa [IsLocalRing.residue_eq_zero_iff] at this
1268	    rw [show x = ∑ i, Pi.single i (x i) from (Finset.univ_sum_single x).symm]
1269	    refine Submodule.sum_mem _ ?_
1270	    intro i _
1271	    have hsingle :
1272	        (Pi.single i (x i) : Fin n → R)
1273	          = (x i) • (Pi.single i (1 : R) : Fin n → R) := by
1274	      ext j; by_cases hij : i = j <;> simp [Pi.single, Function.update, hij]
1275	    rw [hsingle]
1276	    exact Submodule.smul_mem_smul (hx_pi i) trivial

1301	
1302	For a surjection `f : R^n →ₗ M` and a bound `HasProjectiveDimensionLT M (k+2)`

1304	`HasProjectiveDimensionLT K (k+1)`. This is the **per-syzygy** step of the
1305	iter-200 ALIGN_WITH_MATHLIB pivot: the Nat-recursion on `pd` happens entirely at
1306	the Ext-vanishing-class level via `ShortComplex.ShortExact.hasProjectiveDimensionLT_X₁`

1309	lemma hasProjectiveDimensionLT_ker_of_surjection
1310	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R]
1311	    {M : Type u} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M]
1312	    {n : ℕ} (f : (Fin n → R) →ₗ[R] M) (hf : Function.Surjective f)
1313	    {k : ℕ}
1314	    (hM : HasProjectiveDimensionLT (ModuleCat.of R M) (k + 2)) :
1315	    HasProjectiveDimensionLT (ModuleCat.of R (LinearMap.ker f)) (k + 1) := by
1316	  let S := LinearMap.shortComplexKer f
1317	  have hS : S.ShortExact := LinearMap.shortExact_shortComplexKer hf
1318	  haveI hRn_proj : CategoryTheory.Projective (ModuleCat.of R (Fin n → R)) :=
1319	    ModuleCat.projective_of_free (Pi.basisFun R (Fin n))

1321	    hasProjectiveDimensionLT_of_ge _ 1 (k + 1) (by omega)

1328	The companion of `hasProjectiveDimensionLT_ker_of_surjection`: from a syzygy

1330	`HasProjectiveDimensionLT M (k+2)`. Together with the descent, this gives
1331	a clean way to extract `pd K = k+1` exactly (assuming we know
1332	`pd M ≥ k+2`): the contrapositive form is "if `pd K < k+1` then `pd M < k+2`".
1333	This is the input the inductive closure assembly of
1334	`auslander_buchsbaum_formula_succ_pd` needs to extract `pd K = k` exactly. -/
1335	lemma hasProjectiveDimensionLT_succ_of_hasProjectiveDimensionLT_ker
1336	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R]
1337	    {M : Type u} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M]
1338	    {n : ℕ} (f : (Fin n → R) →ₗ[R] M) (hf : Function.Surjective f)
1339	    {k : ℕ}
1340	    (hK_lt : HasProjectiveDimensionLT (ModuleCat.of R (LinearMap.ker f)) (k + 1)) :
1341	    HasProjectiveDimensionLT (ModuleCat.of R M) (k + 2) := by
1342	  let S := LinearMap.shortComplexKer f
1343	  have hS : S.ShortExact := LinearMap.shortExact_shortComplexKer hf
1344	  haveI hRn_proj : CategoryTheory.Projective (ModuleCat.of R (Fin n → R)) :=
1345	    ModuleCat.projective_of_free (Pi.basisFun R (Fin n))
1346	  haveI hRn_pd : HasProjectiveDimensionLT (ModuleCat.of R (Fin n → R)) (k + 2) :=
1347	    hasProjectiveDimensionLT_of_ge _ 1 (k + 2) (by omega)
1348	  exact hS.hasProjectiveDimensionLT_X₃ (k + 1)
1349	    (by simpa [S] using hK_lt) (by simpa [S] using hRn_pd)

1351	/-! ### Helper iter-200 Lane AB-gap1-HasPdLT (axiom-clean): depth lower bound on
1352	the kernel of a surjection from a finite free module.
1353	
1354	For a surjection `f : R^n →ₗ M` from a finite free module of rank `n ≥ 1` over a
1355	Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)`, with both `M` and `ker f` nontrivial, the depth
1356	of `ker f` admits the lower bound
1357	```
1358	  min(depth_𝔪 R, depth_𝔪 M + 1) ≤ depth_𝔪 (ker f).
1359	```
1360	This is Stacks 00LE (3) (`depth_of_short_exact` part 3) applied to the
1361	SES `0 → ker f → R^n → M → 0`, combined with `depth_pi_const_eq_depth_of_nonempty`
1362	to identify `depth(R^n)` with `depth(R)`.
1363	
1364	This is the ≥ direction of the LES analysis on the SES; the matching ≤ direction
1365	(`depth(ker f) ≤ min(depth R, depth M + 1)`) is the residual gap needed for the
1366	inductive step of the Auslander–Buchsbaum formula. It requires either the "what
1367	is exact" criterion (Stacks 00MF) or a refined LES argument when
1368	`depth M < depth R`; the current substrate provides only the easy direction. -/
1369	lemma depth_ker_ge_min_of_surjection_finite_localRing
1370	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
1371	    {M : Type u} [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [_root_.Module.Finite R M] [Nontrivial M]
1372	    {n : ℕ} (hn : 1 ≤ n) (f : (Fin n → R) →ₗ[R] M) (hf : Function.Surjective f)
1373	    [Nontrivial (LinearMap.ker f)] :
1374	    min (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R)
1375	        (depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M + 1)
1376	      ≤ depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) (LinearMap.ker f) := by
1377	  -- Set up instances on the auxiliary modules.
1378	  haveI : Inhabited (Fin n) := ⟨⟨0, hn⟩⟩
1379	  haveI : Nonempty (Fin n) := ⟨default⟩
1380	  haveI : Nontrivial (Fin n → R) := Pi.nontrivial
1381	  haveI : _root_.Module.Finite R (LinearMap.ker f) := Module.IsNoetherian.finite R _
1382	  -- The SES: 0 → ker f → R^n → M → 0.
1383	  have hex : Function.Exact (LinearMap.ker f).subtype f :=
1384	    LinearMap.exact_subtype_ker_map f
1385	  have hinj : Function.Injective (LinearMap.ker f).subtype :=
1386	    Subtype.val_injective
1387	  -- Apply `depth_of_short_exact` part (3).
1388	  have htriple := depth_of_short_exact (LinearMap.ker f).subtype f hinj hf hex
1389	  have h3 := htriple.2.2
1390	  -- Rewrite `depth (Fin n → R) = depth R`.
1391	  have heq : depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) (Fin n → R)
1392	      = depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) R :=
1393	    depth_pi_const_eq_depth_of_nonempty _
1394	  rw [heq] at h3
1395	  exact h3

1397	/-! ### Helper iter-201 Lane AB Path B (axiom-clean): matrix decomposition and
1398	matrix-collapse on `Ext`.
1399	
1400	For an R-linear map `A : R^m →ₗ R^n` between standard free modules over a
1401	commutative ring R, A decomposes as `A = ∑_{(i,j)} A_{i,j} • E_{i,j}` where
1402	`A_{i,j} = (A (Pi.single j 1)) i` is the matrix entry and `E_{i,j}` is the
1403	"elementary" linear map sending `Pi.single j 1 ↦ Pi.single i 1`. From this
1404	decomposition combined with R-bilinearity of `Ext.comp` and the axiom-clean
1405	helper `ext_smul_eq_zero_of_mem_annihilator`, we obtain the matrix-collapse
1406	result: if every entry of A lies in `Ann_R N`, then the induced postcomposition
1407	`Ext^p(N, R^m) → Ext^p(N, R^n)` via `mk₀ (ofHom A)` is the zero map.
1408	
1409	This is the key new substrate for closing the **Path B base case `pd M = 1`**
1410	of the Auslander–Buchsbaum formula: given a minimal surjection `f : R^n ↠ M`
1411	with `ker f` free of positive rank, the inclusion `ker f ≅ R^k ↪ R^n` is an
1412	R-linear map with entries in 𝔪 (by minimality `ker f ≤ 𝔪 • ⊤`); the
1413	matrix-collapse then forces the LES injectivity needed to conclude
1414	`depth M < depth R`. -/
1415	
1416	/-- The "elementary matrix" linear map `E_{i,j} : R^m →ₗ R^n` sending
1417	`Pi.single j 1 ↦ Pi.single i 1` and all other standard basis vectors to 0. -/
1418	private def elemMap {R : Type u} [CommRing R] (n m : ℕ) (i : Fin n) (j : Fin m) :
1419	    (Fin m → R) →ₗ[R] (Fin n → R) :=
1420	  (LinearMap.toSpanSingleton R (Fin n → R) (Pi.single i (1 : R) : Fin n → R)) ∘ₗ
1421	    (LinearMap.proj (R := R) (φ := fun _ : Fin m => R) j)
1422	
1423	/-- The elementary map `E_{i,j}` evaluated at `x : R^m` gives `Pi.single i (x j)`. -/
1424	private lemma elemMap_apply {R : Type u} [CommRing R] (n m : ℕ)
1425	    (i : Fin n) (j : Fin m) (x : Fin m → R) :
1426	    (elemMap n m i j : (Fin m → R) →ₗ[R] (Fin n → R)) x = Pi.single i (x j) := by
1427	  classical
1428	  change (LinearMap.toSpanSingleton R (Fin n → R)
1429	    (Pi.single i (1 : R) : Fin n → R)) (x j) = Pi.single i (x j)
1430	  rw [LinearMap.toSpanSingleton_apply]
1431	  ext k
1432	  rw [Pi.smul_apply]
1433	  by_cases hk : k = i
1434	  · subst hk; rw [Pi.single_eq_same, Pi.single_eq_same, smul_eq_mul, mul_one]
1435	  · rw [Pi.single_eq_of_ne hk, Pi.single_eq_of_ne hk, smul_zero]
1436	
1437	/-- Matrix decomposition: every R-linear map `A : R^m →ₗ R^n` can be written
1438	as a sum `∑_{(i,j)} A (Pi.single j 1) i • elemMap n m i j` of elementary maps
1439	weighted by matrix entries. -/
1440	private lemma linearMap_finFunR_matrix_decomp {R : Type u} [CommRing R] {n m : ℕ}
1441	    (A : (Fin m → R) →ₗ[R] (Fin n → R)) :
1442	    A = ∑ ij : Fin n × Fin m, (A (Pi.single ij.2 1) ij.1) • elemMap n m ij.1 ij.2 := by
1443	  classical
1444	  refine LinearMap.ext fun (x : Fin m → R) => ?_
1445	  rw [LinearMap.sum_apply]
1446	  rw [show A x = ∑ j : Fin m, ∑ i : Fin n,
1447	      (A (Pi.single j 1) i) • (Pi.single i (x j) : Fin n → R) from ?_]
1448	  · rw [← Finset.univ_product_univ, Finset.sum_product_right]
1449	    refine Finset.sum_congr rfl fun j _ => ?_
1450	    refine Finset.sum_congr rfl fun i _ => ?_
1451	    rw [LinearMap.smul_apply, elemMap_apply]
1452	  · have hx_decomp : x = ∑ j : Fin m, (x j) • (Pi.single j (1 : R) : Fin m → R) := by
1453	      ext k
1454	      rw [Finset.sum_apply, Finset.sum_eq_single k]
1455	      · simp
1456	      · intro b _ hb
1457	        rw [Pi.smul_apply, Pi.single_eq_of_ne hb.symm, smul_zero]
1458	      · intro h; exact absurd (Finset.mem_univ k) h
1459	    conv_lhs => rw [hx_decomp, map_sum]
1460	    refine Finset.sum_congr rfl fun j _ => ?_
1461	    rw [map_smul]
1462	    ext k
1463	    rw [Pi.smul_apply, Finset.sum_apply]
1464	    rw [Finset.sum_eq_single k]
1465	    · rw [Pi.smul_apply, Pi.single_eq_same, smul_eq_mul, smul_eq_mul, mul_comm]
1466	    · intro b _ hb
1467	      rw [Pi.smul_apply, Pi.single_eq_of_ne (Ne.symm hb), smul_zero]
1468	    · intro h; exact absurd (Finset.mem_univ k) h
1469	
1470	/-- **Matrix-collapse on Ext.** For an R-linear map `A : R^m →ₗ R^n` whose every
1471	matrix entry `A (Pi.single j 1) i` lies in `Ann_R N`, the postcomposition map
1472	`Ext^p(N, R^m) → Ext^p(N, R^n)` induced by `mk₀ (ofHom A)` is the zero map.
1473	
1474	Proof: write `A = ∑_{(i,j)} A_{i,j} • E_{i,j}` via `linearMap_finFunR_matrix_decomp`.
1475	Push through `ofHom`, `mk₀`, and `Ext.comp` using `ofHom_sum / mk₀_sum / comp_sum`
1476	plus `ofHom_smul / mk₀_smul / comp_smul`. Each summand becomes
1477	`A_{i,j} • (e.comp (mk₀ (ofHom (elemMap _ _ i j))))`, where the scalar `A_{i,j}`
1478	lies in `Ann_R N`. The existing `ext_smul_eq_zero_of_mem_annihilator` (Stacks
1479	00LP fragment) makes each such scalar action zero. Hence the total sum is zero. -/
1480	private lemma ext_comp_mk₀_ofHom_eq_zero_of_entries_mem_annihilator
1481	    {R : Type u} [CommRing R]
1482	    {N : ModuleCat.{u} R}
1483	    {n m : ℕ}
1484	    (A : (Fin m → R) →ₗ[R] (Fin n → R))
1485	    (hA : ∀ (i : Fin n) (j : Fin m),
1486	        A (Pi.single j 1) i ∈ _root_.Module.annihilator R (N : Type u))
1487	    {p : ℕ} (e : Abelian.Ext.{u} N (ModuleCat.of R (Fin m → R)) p) :
1488	    e.comp (CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀
1489	              (ModuleCat.ofHom A :
1490	                ModuleCat.of R (Fin m → R) ⟶ ModuleCat.of R (Fin n → R)))
1491	          (add_zero p) = 0 := by
1492	  classical
1493	  rw [linearMap_finFunR_matrix_decomp A]
1494	  rw [show (ModuleCat.ofHom (∑ ij : Fin n × Fin m,
1495	      A (Pi.single ij.2 1) ij.1 • elemMap n m ij.1 ij.2) :
1496	      ModuleCat.of R (Fin m → R) ⟶ ModuleCat.of R (Fin n → R))
1497	      = ∑ ij : Fin n × Fin m, ModuleCat.ofHom
1498	          (A (Pi.single ij.2 1) ij.1 • elemMap n m ij.1 ij.2) from by
1499	    refine ModuleCat.hom_ext ?_
1500	    rw [ModuleCat.hom_sum]
1501	    rfl]
1502	  rw [CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀_sum]
1503	  rw [CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.comp_sum]
1504	  apply Finset.sum_eq_zero
1505	  intro ij _
1506	  rw [show (ModuleCat.ofHom (A (Pi.single ij.2 1) ij.1 • elemMap n m ij.1 ij.2) :
1507	      ModuleCat.of R (Fin m → R) ⟶ ModuleCat.of R (Fin n → R))
1508	      = A (Pi.single ij.2 1) ij.1 • ModuleCat.ofHom (elemMap n m ij.1 ij.2) from by
1509	    refine ModuleCat.hom_ext ?_; rfl]
1510	  rw [show (CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀
1511	      (A (Pi.single ij.2 1) ij.1 • ModuleCat.ofHom (elemMap n m ij.1 ij.2)) :
1512	        Abelian.Ext.{u} (ModuleCat.of R (Fin m → R)) (ModuleCat.of R (Fin n → R)) 0)
1513	      = A (Pi.single ij.2 1) ij.1 • CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀
1514	          (ModuleCat.ofHom (elemMap n m ij.1 ij.2)) from
1515	      CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.mk₀_smul (R := R) _ _]
1516	  rw [CategoryTheory.Abelian.Ext.comp_smul]
1517	  exact ext_smul_eq_zero_of_mem_annihilator _ (hA ij.1 ij.2)
1518	
1519	end Module

1521	/-! ## §5. The Auslander–Buchsbaum formula
1522	
1523	For a nonzero finite module `M` of finite projective dimension over a
1524	Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)`, the **Auslander–Buchsbaum formula** reads
1525	```
1526	  pd_R(M) + depth(M) = depth(R)
1527	```
1528	(Stacks tag 090V). The proof inducts on `depth(M)`: the base case
1529	`depth(M) = 0` uses a minimal finite free resolution of `M` and the
1530	"what is exact" criterion (Stacks 00MF) plus iterated application of the
1531	depth-on-a-short-exact-sequence lemma (§4) to bound `depth(R)` against the
1532	resolution length; the inductive step picks a common non-zero-divisor
1533	`x ∈ 𝔪` on both `R` and `M`, applies the snake lemma to obtain a minimal
1534	finite free resolution of `M/xM` over `R/xR` of the same length, and uses
1535	the inductive hypothesis on `M/xM` over `R/xR`.
1536	
1537	Blueprint reference: `thm:auslander_buchsbaum` (Stacks tag 090V). -/
1538
